text: Hello, this is Adam Rothermel, Little City Big Town Surgery. I'm presenting here a forty-nine year old male who presented for a routine screening colonoscopy and was found to have this very large seven centimeter nearly obstructing sigmoid colon tumor. Of course, biopsy proved he had no carcinoma, found by our excellent gastroenterologist. Metastatic evaluation was negative for any evidence of distant disease. CEA was normal. And so we proceeded with minimally invasive sigmoid colectomy. I'd just like to say I've benefited so tremendously from so many of you posting your videos, showing your technique, and I just can't tell you how much I've appreciated that and learned from that and how much it's benefited my patients. And so I thought I needed to try to pay it forward and I am doing my best to learn how to edit these videos and narrate, and hopefully somebody can get something from this. But if not, hopefully I can get something from it by receiving your feedback about the operations.

The surgeon begins with a lateral to medial dissection along the white line of Toldt, medializing the left colon and progressing towards the left side of the rectal mesentery. The ureter is identified during this process to ensure safe dissection before proceeding to the medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon flips the sigmoid colon over, elevating the rectal mesentery with assistance to keep it out of the pelvis. The medial to lateral dissection begins, progressing towards the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle.

Clearly I'm using all four of the robotic arms. At this point I have one right arm and then two left arms with the camera to the right of the umbilicus and a little cephalada of the umbilicus on this patient. We've been working with the X-platform for several years. More recently though, we were able to have the benefit of two new XI robots, which has really been wonderful. So we have a good number of surgeons at our institution that do robotics. Most heavily general surgery, but we do have urology and OBGYN as well.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, dissecting it proximal to the takeoff of the superior rectal and left colic arteries. The IMA pedicle appears short, and the surgeon continues to work higher to isolate the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The surgeon releases tension on the vessel before proceeding with the medial to lateral dissection, separating the left colic mesentery from the retroperitoneum. The dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure, providing additional length on the descending colon for the anastomosis.

The surgeon encounters atypical adhesions of the colonica papilloica and omentum at the splenic flexure, carefully dissecting these adhesions to ensure sufficient length. The lateral mobilization is completed up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon selects the proximal resection margin, approximately ten centimeters above the tumor, and clears the mesentery using a vessel sealer. A specimen extraction is performed through a fan and steel incision, slightly larger than five centimeters due to the tumor size. The surgeon uses a purse-string device to place the anvil back in. The rectum is cleared at the distal resection margin, and ICG is utilized to assess perfusion at both the distal and proximal resection margins, confirming good perfusion.

The surgeon uses a blue load on the stapler to transect the rectum, checking the reach before undocking the robot. The anvil is placed, and a 29mm EEA stapler is used to complete the anastomosis from below, with assistance holding the anvil at the bed.
